questions.question: How does blue light from screens affect sleep according to the mechanism-based evidence?

options.text: Blue light heats up the brain, making it harder to fall asleep
Blue light activates intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs) → signals suprachiasmatic nucleus → suppresses melatonin
Blue light damages the retina, causing eye strain that prevents sleep
Blue light has no effect on sleep — it's a myth

questions.explanation: The mechanism is STRONG evidence: Blue light (460-480nm) → ipRGCs → suprachiasmatic nucleus (circadian clock) → melatonin suppression. This is well-established neuroscience, not contested.

questions.question: What is the evidence quality for blue light blocking glasses improving sleep?

options.text: MODERATE-MIXED — some studies show benefit, others show minimal effect
WEAK — no evidence supporting blue light glasses

questions.explanation: While the mechanism is clear, actual sleep outcomes from blue light glasses are MODERATE-MIXED. Some studies show benefit, others show minimal effect. The mechanism is real, but the practical impact of glasses varies.

questions.question: Which intervention has STRONGER evidence for improving sleep than blue light blocking alone?

options.text: Using blue light glasses while scrolling social media in bed
Avoiding screens 1-2 hours before bed (or dimming screens + avoiding arousing content)
Switching all screens to grayscale mode
Using night mode on all devices

questions.explanation: Complete screen avoidance before bed (or combined dim + content strategies) has stronger evidence than blue light blocking alone. The arousal from content (news, social media, work emails) may matter more than blue light wavelength.

questions.question: What time frame does research suggest for blue light suppressing melatonin?

options.text: Only in the first 30 minutes of exposure
Primarily in the 2-3 hours before bedtime, when the circadian system is most sensitive
All day — any blue light exposure ruins sleep
Blue light only affects sleep if you fall asleep with a screen in your hand

questions.explanation: The circadian system is most sensitive to light in the evening (2-3 hours before typical bedtime). Blue light during the day is less problematic and may even support circadian rhythm. Timing matters.

questions.question: How much screen time do you typically have in the 2 hours before bed? What's one specific change you could make to reduce blue light or arousing content exposure during that window?

title: Sleep and Screens: Blue Light Mechanism - Quiz
